themes: adoption gap between capability and usage
infrastructure scaling ahead of user workflows
surprises: Google's internal AI adoption matching John Deere's statistics — same company, completely different tech sophistication.
observations: There's a growing split between teams shipping production multi-agent systems and teams still debating whether AI subscriptions are worth it.
emotional_reactions: Frustrated by the disconnect between impressive technical capabilities and sluggish real-world adoption rates.

per_article_reasoning.title: Anthropic Introduces Routines in Claude Code for Parallel Agents
per_article_reasoning.why: Shows Anthropic betting on multi-agent coordination as the next bottleneck, not just capability.

per_article_reasoning.title: Cursor Speeds Up GPU Kernels by 38% with Multi-Agent System
per_article_reasoning.why: Concrete performance improvement through agent coordination — this is production infrastructure, not a demo.

per_article_reasoning.title: Google's Internal AI Adoption Matches Tractor Companies, Not Tech Leaders
per_article_reasoning.why: Reveals the adoption gap even at companies shipping advanced AI infrastructure themselves.

per_article_reasoning.title: The $7 Doritos Problem: Enterprise AI Subscription Value Crisis
per_article_reasoning.why: Explains why adoption lags behind capability — cost-benefit analysis doesn't work for many teams.

per_article_reasoning.title: LLMs Inherently Lack Laziness: Why AI Makes Systems Larger, Not Better
per_article_reasoning.why: Cantrill's insight about LLMs optimizing for vanity metrics explains some of the adoption resistance.

editorial_note:
Five pieces stood out today, but they're painting a messy picture.

Anthropic shipped routines and multi-agent coordination in Claude Code. They're betting the next bottleneck is agent orchestration,
